name: Marketing & Demand Gen
description: Senior marketing practitioners responsible for pipeline contribution, audience targeting, and campaign performance across ABM, demand gen, and growth motions. They operate at the intersection of strategy and execution, owning the programs that must prove measurable revenue impact.
primary_pain: Disconnected campaigns, stale audiences, and no closed-loop measurement between marketing activity and revenue outcomes

pain_points.title: Audience data goes stale before campaigns launch
pain_points.example: We build a target account list in Q1, spend three weeks getting it approved and loaded into our MAP, and by the time ads are live half the contacts have changed roles or the company has shifted priorities. We're spending budget against a snapshot, not reality.

pain_points.title: No closed-loop visibility from campaign to closed revenue
pain_points.example: I can tell you MQL volume and cost-per-lead all day. What I can't tell you is which campaigns actually contributed to closed-won deals six months later. My CRM data is too dirty and too disconnected from what marketing touched to draw that line with confidence.

pain_points.title: ABM plays require engineering tickets and take weeks to launch
pain_points.example: Every time I want to build a new audience segment or launch an expansion play, I'm filing a ticket with RevOps or waiting on a data analyst to pull a list. By the time it's ready, the intent window has closed. We're always reacting to last quarter's signals.

pain_points.title: Intent signals don't connect to actual buying committee behavior
pain_points.example: We're paying for intent data but it's basically a list of companies that visited some topic pages. I have no idea if the people researching are actually in the buying committee, whether they're early-stage or ready to talk to sales, or whether the signal is even from the right business unit.

pain_points.title: Multi-channel campaigns are stitched together across disconnected tools
pain_points.example: Our paid, email, and SDR sequences are all running off different data sources with different audience definitions. Sales is calling accounts we just suppressed in ads, and we have no single view of what a prospect has actually experienced across channels. It looks coordinated on a slide deck but it's chaos in practice.

pain_points.title: Web traffic is anonymous and form conversion rates are low
pain_points.example: We're driving decent traffic to high-intent pages but our form fill rates are terrible. We ask for ten fields because we need the data for routing, and half the people bounce. The visitors who don't convert are completely invisible to us — we have no idea which accounts are showing up.

goals: Demonstrate measurable pipeline and revenue contribution from marketing programs, not just MQL volume
Launch targeted ABM and demand gen plays faster — in hours or days, not weeks — without depending on engineering or RevOps tickets
Build and maintain audiences that reflect real-time account behavior and buying signals, not static quarterly snapshots
Align marketing campaigns with sales motions so outreach, ads, and sequences hit the same accounts with a coherent message
Increase conversion rates across web forms, landing pages, and digital advertising by reducing friction and improving targeting precision
emotional_state: Skeptical and ROI-fatigued — they've been burned by tools that promised pipeline impact and delivered dashboards, so they're evaluating new platforms through the lens of 'show me the revenue number, not the engagement metric.' They're also acutely aware of the organizational cost of being seen as out of sync with sales — they want to be the team that sales actually wants to work with, not the team that sends campaigns sales ignores. Anything that tightens the feedback loop between marketing activity and sales outcomes, and makes that alignment visible, is a strategic win.
decision_drivers: Ability to prove pipeline and revenue attribution to executive leadership
Speed to launch — reducing the time from insight to live campaign without engineering dependencies
Audience accuracy that reflects current buying behavior, not stale list pulls
Alignment with sales so marketing programs visibly contribute to deals, not just top-of-funnel metrics
Consolidating point solutions into a platform that reduces vendor sprawl and operational overhead
Compliance and data privacy coverage for regulated industries and international campaigns
messaging_angle: Speak to the gap between the campaigns they can build and the revenue proof they're expected to deliver — position ZoomInfo's GTM Context Graph as the intelligence layer that finally closes that loop, and GTM Studio as the execution environment that removes the operational drag between insight and action. Weave in the alignment story too: the best campaign in the world fails if sales doesn't trust the data behind it or can't act on it in their workflow. Position ZoomInfo as the shared operating layer that gets marketing and sales working from the same signals, so campaigns don't just launch — they land.
proof_point_types: Named customer outcomes with specific metrics (Smartsheet: 40%+ form fill increase, 84% MQL increase, 26% opportunity rate increase)
Operational efficiency benchmarks (expansion plays from 3 weeks to 30 minutes)
Engagement and pipeline impact metrics (900% CTR increase, 61% increase in opportunities)
Analyst recognition validating platform category leadership (Gartner Magic Quadrant Leader ABM Platforms 2024 and 2025, Forrester Wave Leader Intent Data Providers Q1 2025)
Scale indicators that establish data foundation credibility (500M contacts, 1.5B+ data points processed daily)
Behavioral benchmarks from top-performing teams (50+ plays per quarter)
tone_modifiers: Revenue-outcome focused — anchor every capability to a pipeline or revenue result, not a feature description
Operationally empathetic — acknowledge the real friction of ticket queues, list-pull delays, and tool fragmentation before presenting the solution
Strategically credible — this persona reads analyst reports and benchmarks peers; cite Gartner, Forrester, and G2 rankings as validation, not decoration
Avoid demand gen jargon as shorthand — terms like 'intent-driven' and 'ABM' need to be grounded in specific mechanics, not used as assumed shared vocabulary
Peer-level confidence — write as a practitioner who understands the difference between MQL theater and real pipeline contribution, not as a vendor pitching features
experience_signals: Content authored or cited by practitioners who have run ABM or demand gen programs at enterprise scale
Examples that reflect real campaign architecture decisions — audience segmentation logic, channel sequencing, suppression lists — not hypothetical use cases
Familiarity with the actual tools in the marketing stack: MAPs, DSPs, CRMs, SEPs, and how data flows between them
Acknowledgment of the organizational dynamics — sales-marketing alignment friction, RevOps dependencies, executive attribution pressure — that shape how decisions actually get made
expertise_signals: Accurate use of ABM and demand gen terminology grounded in how practitioners actually use it, not how vendors define it
Demonstrated understanding of the difference between engagement metrics and pipeline metrics, and why that distinction matters to this persona's credibility internally
Specific knowledge of how intent data works mechanically — topic clusters, IP resolution, behavioral signals — and where it breaks down without a reasoning layer
Awareness of the attribution problem as a structural challenge, not a tooling gap — and positioning ZoomInfo's GTM Context Graph as an intelligence solution, not just another integration
authority_signals: Gartner Magic Quadrant Leader recognition for ABM Platforms (2024 and 2025)
Forrester Wave Leader for Intent Data Providers B2B with highest scores across 8 criteria (Q1 2025)
G2 No. 1 rankings in Buyer Intent, Account Data Management, and Lead-to-Account Matching (Summer 2025)
Named enterprise customers with documented outcomes: Smartsheet, Snowflake, Momentive
Nasdaq ticker change to GTM as a deliberate category ownership signal
trust_signals: Customer outcomes cited with specific named companies, titles, and verifiable metrics — not anonymized case studies
Honest acknowledgment of where point solutions have genuine strengths before explaining where ZoomInfo's integrated approach creates structural advantages
Compliance certifications relevant to enterprise marketing data use: ISO 27001, ISO 27701, SOC 2 Type II, TRUSTe GDPR and CCPA
Proof points sourced from independent analyst evaluations, not self-reported platform claims
Operational specificity — claims like 'expansion plays in 30 minutes vs. 3 weeks' are more trusted than percentage lift claims because they describe a workflow change this persona can visualize
